article.title: Google Adds Reusable AI Skills to Chrome, Turning Gemini Prompts Into One-Click Workflows Across Tabs
article.summary: Chrome's new Skills feature lets users save Gemini prompts and run them across web pages with a single click, rolling out on desktop with a pre-built library of common workflows.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Google is rolling out a new feature called Skills to Gemini in Chrome that turns frequently used AI prompts into saved, reusable workflows that can be triggered with a single click across any web page. The feature, announced on April 14 by Chrome product manager Hafsah Ismail, represents a shift from conversational AI interaction toward embedded, task-oriented automation inside the browser, according to the [Google blog](https://blog.google/products-and-platforms/products/chrome/skills-in-chrome/).

Skills are rolling out on Chrome desktop for Mac, Windows, and ChromeOS, initially limited to users with their Chrome language set to English (US), according to the [Google blog](https://blog.google/products-and-platforms/products/chrome/skills-in-chrome/).

## What We Know

The feature allows users to save any Gemini prompt as a named Skill directly from their chat history. Once saved, a Skill can be activated by typing a forward slash (/) in the Gemini prompt box or by clicking the plus (+) button, according to [9to5Google](https://9to5google.com/2026/04/14/gemini-in-chrome-skills/). The Skill then executes on the current web page, and users can optionally select additional open tabs for the Skill to process simultaneously.

Users can customize their Skills with names and emojis, edit them at any time, and access them across all signed-in Chrome desktop devices, according to [9to5Google](https://9to5google.com/2026/04/14/gemini-in-chrome-skills/). A dedicated management interface is available at chrome://skills/browse, organized into categories including Learning, Research, Shopping, Writing, and Health & Wellness.

Alongside user-created Skills, Google is launching a pre-built Skills library with templates for common tasks. These include product ingredient breakdowns, gift selection with budget and interest cross-referencing, recipe analysis, multi-tab specification comparisons, and document scanning, according to the [Google blog](https://blog.google/products-and-platforms/products/chrome/skills-in-chrome/). Users can add library Skills to their personal collection.

Google said early testers have used Skills for calculating protein macros in recipes, generating side-by-side spec comparisons across multiple tabs, scanning lengthy documents for key information, and creating infographics and visualizations, according to [9to5Google](https://9to5google.com/2026/04/14/gemini-in-chrome-skills/).

On the safety side, Skills use the same safeguards applied to other Gemini actions in Chrome. "Like other Gemini actions in Chrome, Skills will ask the user for confirmation before taking certain actions, like sending an email or adding an event to your calendar," according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/14/google-adds-ai-skills-to-chrome-to-help-you-save-favorite-workflows/). Google also noted that the feature includes automated red-teaming and auto-update capabilities, according to the [Google blog](https://blog.google/products-and-platforms/products/chrome/skills-in-chrome/).

## What We Don't Know

Google has not clarified whether Skills requires a Gemini Advanced subscription or is available to all Chrome users with a Google account. The initial announcement does not address how Skill execution data is stored, retained, or used for model training purposes.

The feature currently works only on desktop and only in English (US), with no timeline given for mobile or multilingual support. It is also unclear how Skills will interact with enterprise Chrome deployments or whether administrators will be able to manage or restrict Skill usage across organizations.

How Skills compares in practice to competing AI browser integrations, such as Microsoft Edge's Copilot sidebar, remains to be seen as the feature rolls out to a broader user base.
